question:
For which real polynomials $p$ is there a real polynomial $q$ such that
\[
p(p(x)) - x = (p(x) - x)^2 q(x)
\]
for all real $x$?
solution:
The answer is $p(x) = \pm x+c$ for any $c \in \mathbb{R}$. Note that any such polynomial works: if $p(x)=x+c$ then $p(x)-x=c$, while if $p(x)=-x+c$ then $p(p(x))-x=0$.
We will show that in fact these are the only polynomials $p(x)$ such that $p(p(x))-x$ is divisible by $r(x)^2$, where $r(x)=p(x)-x$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Suppose that $p(p(x))-x$ is divisible by $r(x)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
x &\equiv p(p(x)) \\
&= p(x + r(x)) \\
&\equiv p(x) + p'(x) r(x) \pmod{r(x)^2}.
\end{align*}
In other words, $r(x) (1 + p'(x))$ is divisible by $r(x)^2$.
From this, it follows that either $r(x) = 0$ or $1+p'(x)$ is divisible by $r(x)$.
In the first case, we have $p(x) = x$.
In the second case, if $1 + p'(x) = 0$ then $p(x) = -x + c$ for some constant $c$;
otherwise, we have
\[
\deg(p) - 1 = \deg(1 + p'(x)) \geq \deg(r)
\]
and this is only possible if $p(x) = x + c$ for some constant $c$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Suppose that $p(p(x))-x$ is divisible by $r(x)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
0 &\equiv \frac{d}{dx}(p(p(x)) - x) \\
&= p'(x) p'(p(x)) - 1 \\
&\equiv p'(x)^2 -1 \\
&= (p'(x) + 1)(p'(x) - 1) \\
&= r'(x) (r'(x) + 2) 
\pmod{r(x)}.
\end{align*}
If $\alpha$ is a root of $r(x)$ of some multiplicity $m$, then the multiplicity of $\alpha$ as a root of $r'(x)$ is $m-1$. Consequently, every root of $r(x)$ must be a root of $r'(x)+ 2$;
in particular such a root cannot also be a root of $r'(x)$, so every root of $r(x)$ is simple.
Putting this together, we deduce that $r(x)$ divides $r'(x) + 2$. If $r(x)$ is constant,
then $p(x) = x+c$ for some $c$. Otherwise, $\deg(r'(x) + 2) < \deg(r(x))$ and so $r'(x) + 2$ must be zero; then $r(x) = -2x + c$ for some $c$, whence $p(x) = -x + c$.

descriptive_long.question:
For which real polynomials polyfunc is there a real polynomial quotient such that
\[
polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput)) - varinput = (polyfunc(varinput) - varinput)^2 quotient(varinput)
\]
for all real varinput?
descriptive_long.solution:
The answer is polyfunc(varinput) = \pm varinput+shiftval for any shiftval \in \mathbb{R}. Note that any such polynomial works: if polyfunc(varinput)=varinput+shiftval then polyfunc(varinput)-varinput=shiftval, while if polyfunc(varinput)=-varinput+shiftval then polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput))-varinput=0.
We will show that in fact these are the only polynomials polyfunc(varinput) such that polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput))-varinput is divisible by residual(varinput)^2, where residual(varinput)=polyfunc(varinput)-varinput. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Suppose that polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput))-varinput is divisible by residual(varinput)^2. Then
\begin{align*}
varinput &\equiv polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput)) \\
&= polyfunc(varinput + residual(varinput)) \\
&\equiv polyfunc(varinput) + polyfunc'(varinput) residual(varinput) \pmod{residual(varinput)^2}.
\end{align*}
In other words, residual(varinput) (1 + polyfunc'(varinput)) is divisible by residual(varinput)^2.
From this, it follows that either residual(varinput) = 0 or 1+polyfunc'(varinput) is divisible by residual(varinput).
In the first case, we have polyfunc(varinput) = varinput.
In the second case, if 1 + polyfunc'(varinput) = 0 then polyfunc(varinput) = -varinput + shiftval for some shiftval;
otherwise, we have
\[
\deg(polyfunc) - 1 = \deg(1 + polyfunc'(varinput)) \geq \deg(residual)
\]
and this is only possible if polyfunc(varinput) = varinput + shiftval for some shiftval.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Suppose that polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput))-varinput is divisible by residual(varinput)^2. Then
\begin{align*}
0 &\equiv \frac{d}{dvarinput}(polyfunc(polyfunc(varinput)) - varinput) \\
&= polyfunc'(varinput) polyfunc'(polyfunc(varinput)) - 1 \\
&\equiv polyfunc'(varinput)^2 -1 \\
&= (polyfunc'(varinput) + 1)(polyfunc'(varinput) - 1) \\
&= residual'(varinput) (residual'(varinput) + 2) 
\pmod{residual(varinput)}.
\end{align*}
If rootalpha is a root of residual(varinput) of some multiplicity multiplicity, then the multiplicity of rootalpha as a root of residual'(varinput) is multiplicity-1. Consequently, every root of residual(varinput) must be a root of residual'(varinput)+ 2;
in particular such a root cannot also be a root of residual'(varinput), so every root of residual(varinput) is simple.
Putting this together, we deduce that residual(varinput) divides residual'(varinput) + 2. If residual(varinput) is constant,
then polyfunc(varinput) = varinput+shiftval for some shiftval. Otherwise, \deg(residual'(varinput) + 2) < \deg(residual(varinput)) and so residual'(varinput) + 2 must be zero; then residual(varinput) = -2varinput + shiftval for some shiftval, whence polyfunc(varinput) = -varinput + shiftval.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For which real polynomials $sunflower$ is there a real polynomial $lighthouse$ such that
\[
sunflower(sunflower(candlestick)) - candlestick = (sunflower(candlestick) - candlestick)^2 lighthouse(candlestick)
\]
for all real $candlestick$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The answer is $sunflower(candlestick) = \pm candlestick+cookbook$ for any $cookbook \in \mathbb{R}$. Note that any such polynomial works: if $sunflower(candlestick)=candlestick+cookbook$ then $sunflower(candlestick)-candlestick=cookbook$, while if $sunflower(candlestick)=-candlestick+cookbook$ then $sunflower(sunflower(candlestick))-candlestick=0$.
We will show that in fact these are the only polynomials $sunflower(candlestick)$ such that $sunflower(sunflower(candlestick))-candlestick$ is divisible by $hummingbird(candlestick)^2$, where $hummingbird(candlestick)=sunflower(candlestick)-candlestick$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Suppose that $sunflower(sunflower(candlestick))-candlestick$ is divisible by $hummingbird(candlestick)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
candlestick &\equiv sunflower(sunflower(candlestick)) \\
&= sunflower(candlestick + hummingbird(candlestick)) \\
&\equiv sunflower(candlestick) + sunflower'(candlestick) hummingbird(candlestick) \pmod{hummingbird(candlestick)^2}.
\end{align*}
In other words, $hummingbird(candlestick) (1 + sunflower'(candlestick))$ is divisible by $hummingbird(candlestick)^2$.
From this, it follows that either $hummingbird(candlestick) = 0$ or $1+sunflower'(candlestick)$ is divisible by $hummingbird(candlestick)$.
In the first case, we have $sunflower(candlestick) = candlestick$.
In the second case, if $1 + sunflower'(candlestick) = 0$ then $sunflower(candlestick) = -candlestick + cookbook$ for some constant $cookbook$;
otherwise, we have
\[
\deg(sunflower) - 1 = \deg(1 + sunflower'(candlestick)) \geq \deg(hummingbird)
\]
and this is only possible if $sunflower(candlestick) = candlestick + cookbook$ for some constant $cookbook$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Suppose that $sunflower(sunflower(candlestick))-candlestick$ is divisible by $hummingbird(candlestick)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
0 &\equiv \frac{d}{dcandlestick}(sunflower(sunflower(candlestick)) - candlestick) \\
&= sunflower'(candlestick) sunflower'(sunflower(candlestick)) - 1 \\
&\equiv sunflower'(candlestick)^2 -1 \\
&= (sunflower'(candlestick) + 1)(sunflower'(candlestick) - 1) \\
&= hummingbird'(candlestick) (hummingbird'(candlestick) + 2) 
\pmod{hummingbird(candlestick)}.
\end{align*}
If $marzipan$ is a root of $hummingbird(candlestick)$ of some multiplicity $rainstorm$, then the multiplicity of $marzipan$ as a root of $hummingbird'(candlestick)$ is $rainstorm-1$. Consequently, every root of $hummingbird(candlestick)$ must be a root of $hummingbird'(candlestick)+ 2$;
in particular such a root cannot also be a root of $hummingbird'(candlestick)$, so every root of $hummingbird(candlestick)$ is simple.
Putting this together, we deduce that $hummingbird(candlestick)$ divides $hummingbird'(candlestick) + 2$. If $hummingbird(candlestick)$ is constant,
then $sunflower(candlestick) = candlestick+cookbook$ for some $cookbook$. Otherwise, $\deg(hummingbird'(candlestick) + 2) < \deg(hummingbird(candlestick))$ and so $hummingbird'(candlestick) + 2$ must be zero; then $hummingbird(candlestick) = -2candlestick + cookbook$ for some $cookbook$, whence $sunflower(candlestick) = -candlestick + cookbook$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For which real polynomials $constantmap$ is there a real polynomial $staticform$ such that
\[
constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue)) - knownvalue = (constantmap(knownvalue) - knownvalue)^2 staticform(knownvalue)
\]
for all real $knownvalue$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The answer is $constantmap(knownvalue) = \pm knownvalue+variable$ for any $variable \in \mathbb{R}$. Note that any such polynomial works: if $constantmap(knownvalue)=knownvalue+variable$ then $constantmap(knownvalue)-knownvalue=variable$, while if $constantmap(knownvalue)=-knownvalue+variable$ then $constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue))-knownvalue=0$.
We will show that in fact these are the only polynomials $constantmap(knownvalue)$ such that $constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue))-knownvalue$ is divisible by $sumvalue(knownvalue)^2$, where $sumvalue(knownvalue)=constantmap(knownvalue)-knownvalue$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Suppose that $constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue))-knownvalue$ is divisible by $sumvalue(knownvalue)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
knownvalue &\equiv constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue)) \\
&= constantmap(knownvalue + sumvalue(knownvalue)) \\
&\equiv constantmap(knownvalue) + constantmap'(knownvalue) sumvalue(knownvalue) \pmod{sumvalue(knownvalue)^2}.
\end{align*}
In other words, $sumvalue(knownvalue) (1 + constantmap'(knownvalue))$ is divisible by $sumvalue(knownvalue)^2$.
From this, it follows that either $sumvalue(knownvalue) = 0$ or $1+constantmap'(knownvalue)$ is divisible by $sumvalue(knownvalue)$.
In the first case, we have $constantmap(knownvalue) = knownvalue$.
In the second case, if $1 + constantmap'(knownvalue) = 0$ then $constantmap(knownvalue) = -knownvalue + variable$ for some constant $variable$;
otherwise, we have
\[
\deg(constantmap) - 1 = \deg(1 + constantmap'(knownvalue)) \geq \deg(sumvalue)
\]
and this is only possible if $constantmap(knownvalue) = knownvalue + variable$ for some constant $variable$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Suppose that $constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue))-knownvalue$ is divisible by $sumvalue(knownvalue)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
0 &\equiv \frac{d}{dknownvalue}(constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue)) - knownvalue) \\
&= constantmap'(knownvalue) constantmap'(constantmap(knownvalue)) - 1 \\
&\equiv constantmap'(knownvalue)^2 -1 \\
&= (constantmap'(knownvalue) + 1)(constantmap'(knownvalue) - 1) \\
&= sumvalue'(knownvalue) (sumvalue'(knownvalue) + 2) 
\pmod{sumvalue(knownvalue)}.
\end{align*}
If $nonrooted$ is a root of $sumvalue(knownvalue)$ of some multiplicity $unityonly$, then the multiplicity of $nonrooted$ as a root of $sumvalue'(knownvalue)$ is $unityonly-1$. Consequently, every root of $sumvalue(knownvalue)$ must be a root of $sumvalue'(knownvalue)+ 2$;
in particular such a root cannot also be a root of $sumvalue'(knownvalue)$, so every root of $sumvalue(knownvalue)$ is simple.
Putting this together, we deduce that $sumvalue(knownvalue)$ divides $sumvalue'(knownvalue) + 2$. If $sumvalue(knownvalue)$ is constant,
then $constantmap(knownvalue) = knownvalue+variable$ for some $variable$. Otherwise, $\deg(sumvalue'(knownvalue) + 2) < \deg(sumvalue(knownvalue))$ and so $sumvalue'(knownvalue) + 2$ must be zero; then $sumvalue(knownvalue) = -2knownvalue + variable$ for some $variable$, whence $constantmap(knownvalue) = -knownvalue + variable$.

garbled_string.question:
For which real polynomials $hjgrksla$ is there a real polynomial $nvdalwqe$ such that
\[
hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)) - qzxwvtnp = (hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) - qzxwvtnp)^2 nvdalwqe(qzxwvtnp)
\]
for all real $qzxwvtnp$?
garbled_string.solution:
The answer is $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = \pm qzxwvtnp+wqjzhekt$ for any $wqjzhekt \in \mathbb{R}$. Note that any such polynomial works: if $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp+wqjzhekt$ then $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)-qzxwvtnp=wqjzhekt$, while if $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=-qzxwvtnp+wqjzhekt$ then $hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp))-qzxwvtnp=0$.
We will show that in fact these are the only polynomials $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)$ such that $hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp))-qzxwvtnp$ is divisible by $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)^2$, where $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)=hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)-qzxwvtnp$.

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
Suppose that $hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp))-qzxwvtnp$ is divisible by $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp &\equiv hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)) \\
&= hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp + tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)) \\
&\equiv hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) + hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp) \pmod{tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)^2}.
\end{align*}
In other words, $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp) (1 + hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp))$ is divisible by $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)^2$.
From this, it follows that either $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp) = 0$ or $1+hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)$ is divisible by $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)$.
In the first case, we have $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = qzxwvtnp$.
In the second case, if $1 + hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) = 0$ then $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = -qzxwvtnp + wqjzhekt$ for some constant $wqjzhekt$;
otherwise, we have
\[
\deg(hjgrksla) - 1 = \deg(1 + hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)) \geq \deg(tczmpsio)
\]
and this is only possible if $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = qzxwvtnp + wqjzhekt$ for some constant $wqjzhekt$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Suppose that $hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp))-qzxwvtnp$ is divisible by $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)^2$. Then
\begin{align*}
0 &\equiv \frac{d}{dqzxwvtnp}(hjgrksla(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)) - qzxwvtnp) \\
&= hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) hjgrksla'(hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)) - 1 \\
&\equiv hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp)^2 -1 \\
&= (hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) + 1)(hjgrksla'(qzxwvtnp) - 1) \\
&= tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp) (tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp) + 2) 
\pmod{tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)}.
\end{align*}
If $vodkpmrq$ is a root of $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)$ of some multiplicity $cztbhnla$, then the multiplicity of $vodkpmrq$ as a root of $tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp)$ is $cztbhnla-1$. Consequently, every root of $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)$ must be a root of $tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp)+ 2$;
in particular such a root cannot also be a root of $tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp)$, so every root of $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)$ is simple.
Putting this together, we deduce that $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)$ divides $tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp) + 2$. If $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp)$ is constant,
then $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = qzxwvtnp+wqjzhekt$ for some $wqjzhekt$. Otherwise, $\deg(tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp) + 2) < \deg(tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp))$ and so $tczmpsio'(qzxwvtnp) + 2$ must be zero; then $tczmpsio(qzxwvtnp) = -2qzxwvtnp + wqjzhekt$ for some $wqjzhekt$, whence $hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) = -qzxwvtnp + wqjzhekt$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let \(p\in\mathbb{C}[x]\) be a complex-coefficient polynomial.  Determine all such \(p\) for which there exists another complex polynomial \(q\) satisfying
\[
p\bigl(p(x)\bigr)-x\;=
\bigl(p(x)-x\bigr)^{\mathbf 3}\,q(x)
\qquad\text{for every }x\in\mathbb{C}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Let p\in \mathbb{C}[x] and set r(x)=p(x)-x.  We seek all p for which
  p(p(x))-x  is divisible by  r(x)^3.

1.  Degree considerations.  Write d=deg p.  If d=0 then p(x)=constant=k, so r(x)=k-x has degree 1 and p(p(x))-x=k-x=r(x), which is not divisible by r(x)^3 unless r\equiv 0.  But r\equiv 0 means k-x\equiv 0, impossible.  Hence d\geq 1.

2.  Taylor-expansion modulo r^3.  Since p(p(x))=p(x+r(x)), one has formally
  p(p(x)) = p(x)+p'(x)r(x)+\frac{1}{2} p''(x)r(x)^2 + O(r(x)^3).
Subtracting x=(p(x)-r(x)) gives
  p(p(x))-x = r(x)[1+p'(x)] + \frac{1}{2} p''(x)r(x)^2 + O(r(x)^3).
If this is divisible by r(x)^3, then the coefficients of r(x)^1 and r(x)^2 must themselves be divisible by r(x).  Equivalently,
  r(x) | (1+p'(x)),
  r(x) | p''(x).

3.  If r is constant, then p(x)-x=c, so p(x)=x+c and obviously p(p(x))-x=2c=(p(x)-x)^3\cdot q(x) with q(x)=2/c^2 (or arbitrary if c=0).  Thus p(x)=x+c is a solution.

4.  If r is nonconstant then deg r=deg p=d.  From r\mid [1+p'] we get
  d = deg r \leq  deg(1+p') = deg p' = d-1,
 a contradiction unless 1+p'(x)\equiv 0 identically.  Hence p'(x)=-1 and p(x)=-x+c.  In that case p(p(x))=x and so p(p(x))-x\equiv 0, which is divisible by any power of r(x)=-2x+c; thus p(x)=-x+c also works.

5.  Conclusion.  The only complex-coefficient solutions are
  p(x)=x+c  or  p(x)=-x+c  (c\in \mathbb{C}),
and one checks directly that each indeed makes p(p(x))-x a multiple of (p(x)-x)^3.

core_steps: Let r(x)=p(x)−x so that r(x)^2 divides p(p(x))−x
Use Taylor expansion: p(p(x))=p(x+r)≡p(x)+p'(x)r(x) (mod r(x)^2), hence p(p(x))−x≡r(x)(1+p'(x))
Divisibility gives r(x)(1+p'(x)) divisible by r(x)^2 ⇒ either r(x)=0 or r(x) | 1+p'(x)
If r(x)=0 then p(x)=x+c; if 1+p'(x)≡0 then p(x)=−x+c
Otherwise degree comparison forces r(x) constant, again yielding p(x)=x+c

slot1.description: Underlying coefficient field of the polynomials

slot2.description: Exponent in the divisibility requirement (currently squared)
